sections.title: What modals do (core logic)
sections.content:
Modal verbs add a layer of **speaker stance** to the main verb — necessity, ability, possibility, advice, permission.
- They tell the listener how the speaker sees the action.
- Tenses locate events in time; modals colour them with the speaker's attitude — the modality dimension of the Verb Phrase root (#2 in the Tree Model).
- Every workplace email, polite request, exam essay and real conversation runs on them — without them English sounds robotic.

sections.title: Form: the three rules that never change
sections.content: Three form rules that never change, whatever the modal.

items.why: base form of the main verb follows — no *to*, no *-ing*, no past tense

diagrams.text: *můžu, musím, měl bych* often map to modals, but the form rules are different — Czech conjugates the modal (*musíš, musíme*), English does not.

sections.title: Ability & permission: can / could / be able to
sections.content:
**Ability** = what someone is capable of. **Permission** = what someone is allowed to do.
- *Can I?* / *Could I?* / *May I?* is a gradient from casual to formal — asking politely is one of the most visible markers of fluent English; students who only have *can* sound abrupt in work and exam contexts.
- *can* covers present only — the moment you need future, perfect or *-ing*, you must use **be able to**.

diagrams.title: Ability & permission
rows: I **could** swim when I was six.

diagrams.title: can has no future or perfect form

items.label: future
items.formula: will be able to → She will be able to come tomorrow.

items.label: perfect
items.formula: have/has been able to → He has been able to finish.

sections.title: Necessity & obligation: must / have to / mustn't / don't have to
sections.content:
The highest-error area for Czech speakers: four forms, four distinct meanings.

*mustn't* and *don't have to* look like opposites of the same thing but mean completely different things — in a workplace email, "You mustn't reply" ≠ "you don't have to reply".

items.why: internal / speaker-decided — I feel I should

items.good: I **have to** wear a uniform at work.
You **don't have to** come — it's optional.
items.why: no obligation — you can if you want

sections.title: Form: have to vs true modals
sections.content: **have to** behaves like a normal verb — it takes *do/does/did* in questions and negatives, unlike true modals.

diagrams.title: have to as a normal verb

diagrams.text: *musím* maps to both *must* and *have to* depending on context. *Nemusím* is **don't have to** (no obligation), NOT *mustn't*.

sections.title: Advice: should / ought to / had better
sections.content:
Giving advice is everywhere — three forms on a gradient of **urgency**. The wrong level sounds either bossy or weak.

**had better** is much stronger than students realise — it implies a threat or warning, not gentle advice.

diagrams.title: Advice by urgency
rows: urgent — warns of a bad consequence
You'**d better** leave now or you'll miss the train.

items.why: + base form; the contraction *'d better* is normal

sections.title: Speculation & deduction — present: must / might / could / can't
sections.content:
Modals also express how **certain** the speaker is about something now — guessing, deducing, hedging. One of the most useful skills at B1+.
- All take the **base form**: *must be, might rain, could work, can't know*.
- The heart of natural conversation: "He **must** be tired," "That **might** work," "She **can't** be serious." FCE/CAE speaking rewards hedging with *might/could* over flat assertions.

items.good: She **must** be at home — her car's outside.
She **might / may / could** be at home — I'm not sure.
She **can't** be at home — she's in Paris.

diagrams.label: Trap
diagrams.text: For negative deduction, the opposite of **must** (certain) is **can't** (certain not) — NOT *mustn't*. *Mustn't* is prohibition, not deduction.

sections.title: Speculation & deduction — past: must have / might have / can't have
sections.content:
Same logic as present deduction, but about something that already happened: **modal + have + past participle**.

This structure separates B1 from B2 speakers — native English uses it constantly to reason about past events, and FCE/CAE key word transformations test it heavily.

items.good: She **must have** left early — her coat's gone.
He **might / may / could have** missed the train.
They **can't have** finished already — it's only been an hour.

items.why: regret — I didn't, but I wish I had

diagrams.label: Trap
diagrams.text: It's *must **have** done* — the *have* is required. Speech contracts it to *must've /ˈmʌstəv/*, which Czech learners often hear as *must of* and write incorrectly. It's always **have**.

sections.title: Past forms: what replaces each modal
sections.content: Most modals have **no past form** — you switch to a different verb or structure.

diagrams.title: Past equivalents
rows: I **was able to** finish the report on time.

sections.title: Top errors Czech speakers make
sections.content:
These patterns cover the vast majority of modal errors in marked work — and they're all checked in every Use of English paper.

Fixing them gives an immediate, visible level-up in writing and speaking. For full modal reference, see **Reference** → **Modal Verbs**.

questions.question: What is **modality** in grammar?

options.a: The time of the action (past, present, future)
options.b: The way we express possibility, necessity, permission, advice, etc.
options.c: The person (I, you, he/she) of the subject

questions.explanation: **Modality** is how we express meanings like possibility, necessity, permission, ability, obligation, or advice – the 'layer' of meaning added by modals and semi-modals.

questions.question: Which sentence uses a **semi-modal**?

options.b: You ought to see a doctor.

questions.explanation: **Ought to** is a semi-modal (followed by **to** + base verb). **Can**, **must**, and **might** are core modals (no *to*).

questions.question: 'You ____ apologise.' (strong advice, formal) Which fits best?
questions.explanation: **Ought to** expresses advice or moral obligation, often in a more formal tone. *Should* is also possible but *ought to* fits 'formal' best here.

questions.question: Which of these uses 'to' before the main verb? (e.g. ___ to go)
questions.explanation: **Ought to** always includes 'to' before the main verb: we say 'ought to go', 'ought to see'. **Must**, **should**, and **can** are followed directly by the base form (must go, should see, can go).

questions.question: For external obligation (e.g. a rule or someone else's requirement), we often use:
questions.explanation: **Have to** is commonly used for external obligation (rules, boss, law). **Must** can express the speaker's own obligation or a strong rule; **have to** is typical when the obligation comes from outside.

questions.question: Which verb do we use for **ability** when we need a form that *can* doesn't have (e.g. future)?
questions.explanation: **Be able to** is used for ability when we need another tense or structure, e.g. *She will be able to come*, *I have been able to finish*. **Can** has no future or perfect form.

gapfill.title: Practice: Choose the modal or semi-modal

questions.question: You ____ (advice) see a doctor if your headache continues. (ought to / should)
She ____ speak three languages. (ability)
questions.explanation: Ability in the present → **can** + base verb.

questions.question: We ____ wear a uniform at school. (external rule)
It ____ rain later, so take an umbrella. (possibility)
You ____ (not) tell anyone – it's a secret. (obligation not to)
questions.explanation: Obligation not to do something → **must not** or **cannot**.

questions.question: He ____ give up smoking. (advice, semi-modal)
questions.explanation: Advice with the semi-modal → **ought to** + base verb.
